domains.question: Is a one-human studio panel valid for an alt-test assuming annotator independence?

findings.claim: The alt-test does NOT assume annotator independence; it assumes a representative human consensus tested via leave-one-out (paired t-test + FDR). 3+ required (two only checks alignment). Epsilon: expert 0.2, skilled 0.15, crowd 0.1. Correlated raters bias omega and the FDR toward false confidence, since correlated folds look more significant than independent ones. A few genuinely independent non-experts recover expert quality once pooled (~four on affect tasks); the leverage is independent errors cancelling, destroyed when raters share context.
findings.source: Calderon, Reichart, Dror 2025, Alternative Annotator Test for LLM-as-a-Judge, ACL 2025, arXiv:2501.10970, https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.10970 ; README https://github.com/nitaytech/AltTest ; Benjamini, Yekutieli 2001, Annals of Statistics 29(4):1165-1188, https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1013699998 ; Snow, OConnor, Jurafsky, Ng 2008, Cheap and Fast But is it Good, EMNLP 2008, https://aclanthology.org/D08-1027/
findings.design_implication: MIN_ANNOTATORS=3 and TIER_EPSILON are correct; reframe 12.2 so non-circularity rests on humans-not-models, not uncorrelated annotators. If director plus two crew make the same calls the folds are near-duplicates and omega inflates; keep Benjamini-Yekutieli (valid under dependency) and require >=1 out-of-studio reviewer before flipping a seat PROVISIONAL to SEATED. Recruit 1-2 external reviewers (rubric+items only, no rationale); internal-only crew tier crowd at epsilon 0.1, not expert.

findings.claim: Shared context inflates apparent agreement without improving correctness: annotators defer to the primary and shared-guideline training creates artificial convergence overestimating reliability. Krippendorff alpha and kappa are destabilized by the prevalence skew correlated raters create (kappa paradox: 195/200 agreement yields kappa 0.747); Gwet AC1 is less prevalence-sensitive. High disagreement is signal not error, so DISPUTED items should be dropped, not force-resolved.
findings.source: Cook et al. 2025, Efficient Annotator Reliability Assessment with EffiARA, arXiv:2504.00589, https://arxiv.org/html/2504.00589v3 ; Wongpakaran et al. 2013, BMC Med Res Methodol 13:61, https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2288-13-61 ; Zec et al. 2023, https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37234937/ ; Aroyo, Welty 2015, AI Magazine 36(1):15-24, https://doi.org/10.1609/aimag.v36i1.2564 ; Plank 2022, arXiv:2211.02570
findings.design_implication: Deference inflates Krippendorff alpha, relaxing the epsilon clamp (alpha>=0.667 keeps tier epsilon) and shrinking disputes. Mandatory: separate sessions, no shared rationale, no cross-visibility; persist a per-annotator isolation attestation; report Gwet AC1 beside alpha; balance the gold marginal to half/half; if they disagree prefer epsilon 0.1. Validates DISPUTE_FLOOR=0.67; a low disputed-rate with internal-only annotators is a convergence red flag, so add known-ambiguous attention checks.

domains.recommendation: Valid only if independence is enforced at elicitation. Seat = director plus >=1 external reviewer plus one more, three minimum. Safeguards in findings.
domains.uncertainty: Correlated-folds-inflate-omega is my inference, not a quantified paper result. No numeric threshold exists for too-correlated at three raters.</uncertainty>

domains.question: ai-crucible Epic 3 epsilon and power for a 3-5 expert alt-test panel
domains.recommendation: Keep tier epsilon (expert 0.2), a cost knob not a power knob; do not shrink for small N. 3x30 is the floor; target 4-5 raters x 50-100 items; add per-fold power and a bootstrap CI on alpha.
domains.uncertainty: Effect size unknown until a pilot (3 raters x ~20 items). Calderon counts inferred from Fig 2. BY-vs-Bonferroni at m<=5 and Wilcoxon small-N power not benchmarked here.

findings.claim: Epsilon is set by annotator QUALITY not panel size (expert 0.2, skilled 0.15, crowd 0.1); min 3 annotators and 30 items (50-100 sufficient), so 3x30 is the FLOOR. Sample size, not epsilon, governs confidence.
findings.source: Calderon, Reichart, Dror 2025, Alternative Annotator Test, arXiv:2501.10970 (ACL 2025).
findings.design_implication: Keep per-tier epsilon and 3/30 hard gate; declare reviewers expert (0.2); do not shrink for small N; target 50-100 items, 4-5 raters; author hard items above the 28 IRT survivors.

findings.claim: Paired t-test at N=30 has modest power (adequate only for large effects); epsilon 0.2 shifts the null and raises power; BY at m=3-5 folds divides BH by harmonic c(m) and is deliberately conservative.
findings.source: UCLA OARC G-Power; Benjamini & Yekutieli 2001, Annals of Statistics 29(4) 1165-1188.
findings.design_implication: Report per-fold effect size + power from the diffs vector (metrics.alt_test ~lines 566-575) and raw per-fold p + c(m); both push toward 4-5 raters x 50+ items; guard against passed-but-every-fold-marginal.

domains.question: What is the leanest elicitation surface that still satisfies the ai-crucible annotation contract (A/B with a real "unsure", randomized + persisted item order, blinded to gold, embedded attention/gold-check items)? Is a CSV/JSON round-trip sufficient, or do blinding / order-persistence / attention-checking demand a thin form/app? What ai-crucible label-export/ingest glue should Epic 3 build, and what must it enforce?

findings.claim: Randomized presentation order is the field-standard bias control for A/B human eval, and the A/B assignment must be randomized independently per comparison (ideally per annotator) -- but the order ACTUALLY SHOWN must be recorded, because position/order bias moves outcomes and scales with the quality gap. Randomization conflicts with active learning, but ai-crucible has no active-learning loop, so it is pure upside.
findings.source: Shi et al. 2024, 'Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge' (arXiv:2406.07791, https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07791); corroborated by Eckman, Plank & Kreuter 2024, 'Position: Insights from Survey Methodology can Improve Training Data' (ICML 2024, arXiv:2403.01208, https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.01208), which recommends randomizing observation order to defeat contrast and fatigue order effects.
findings.design_implication: The EXPORTER owns randomization, not the human and not the ingester. For each (annotator, item) draw and PERSIST shown_order in {AB, BA} into the export manifest, render candidates in that order, then strip any which-side-is-correct hint. The ingester inverts shown_order before calling _to_num to recover the true verdict. This makes order persistence a machine guarantee rather than human discipline -- a CSV the human can re-sort by hand silently voids it. The round-trip artifact must therefore carry a per-row shown_order that the human never edits and the ingester always reads, which also lets position_bias run on the humans.

findings.claim: A genuine 'don't know'/middle option is nearly free insurance: when offered to half of annotators, fewer than 3% chose it and the overall label distribution was unaffected; and when annotators DO flag uncertainty, those items are exactly where models also struggle -- so 'unsure' carries signal rather than noise. This empirically validates ai-crucible already treating unsure/tie as no-label.
findings.source: Eckman, Plank & Kreuter 2024 (arXiv:2403.01208) summarizing Beck et al. 2022 (the <3% don't-know finding) and Bassignana & Plank 2022 (uncertain flags track model difficulty by genre); contract grounding Krosnick & Presser 2010 (forced binary injects ~14% acquiescence).
findings.design_implication: Keep the contract's no-coerce 'unsure' but make it a FIRST-CLASS, single-keystroke choice in the artifact (e.g. a verdict column whose legal values are A / B / unsure, validated on ingest). Do NOT bury it -- the <3% rate means it costs almost no items while preserving the DISPUTED/ambiguity signal the loader already drops from omega. Ingest must reject any verdict value outside the closed set rather than silently coercing a typo to a side (human_labels.py already does this via _KNOWN_VERDICTS; the exporter/UI must mirror the same closed enum so the round-trip cannot produce an out-of-set value in the first place).

findings.claim: Embedded gold/known-answer attention checks (a.k.a. 'instructed response items' / test observations) are the standard mechanism to detect speeders and satisficers, and the right unit of per-annotator quality gating; implicit checks reuse items on which annotators previously fully agreed. Aggregated non-expert labels can only approach expert quality WITH such controls in place.
findings.source: Daniel et al. 2018, 'Quality Control in Crowdsourcing: A Survey of Quality Attributes, Assessment Techniques, and Assurance Actions' (ACM Computing Surveys, doi:10.1145/3148148, https://doi.org/10.1145/3148148); Snow et al. 2008, 'Cheap and Fast -- But is it Good?' (EMNLP 2008, https://aclanthology.org/D08-1027/); survey-methodology framing of 'instructed response items' in Eckman, Plank & Kreuter 2024 (arXiv:2403.01208).
findings.design_implication: The EXPORTER must inject a configurable fraction of gold-known catch items (items with an unambiguous correct side), interleaved at randomized positions and INDISTINGUISHABLE from real items in the artifact (no marker column the human can see). Keep a private mapping {row_id -> is_catch, expected_side} that the human never receives. On ingest, compute each annotator's catch-pass rate and gate: below a configurable floor, drop or flag that annotator before they enter records_per_annotator. This is the one piece a plain hand-filled CSV cannot guarantee, because the human would see the catch items in the same file; it pushes toward either (a) two-file split-knowledge export (public items file + private answer key) or (b) a thin form that holds the key server-side.

findings.claim: Disagreement is not automatically error: isolated one-off disagreements signal attention lapses (catchable by attention checks), category-level disagreement signals unclear guidelines, and persistent expert disagreement marks genuine task ambiguity that should be preserved, not force-resolved. Annotation should be modeled as measurement, with quality controls separating these cases.
findings.source: Aroyo & Welty 2015, 'Truth Is a Lie: Crowd Truth and the Seven Myths of Human Annotation' (AI Magazine 36(1):15-24, doi:10.1609/aimag.v36i1.2564, https://ojs.aaai.org/aimagazine/index.php/aimagazine/article/view/2564); reinforced by the PLOS One 2024 sentiment-annotation study showing instruction clarity drives agreement.
findings.design_implication: The ingest report must distinguish the three disagreement classes mechanically: attention-check failures (per-annotator gate, already implied), low Krippendorff alpha overall (guideline problem -> the loader already clamps epsilon and flags 'add items'), and per-item DISPUTED drops (genuine ambiguity, already excluded from the omega denominator). Surface a per-item disagreement breakdown in the ingest summary so the director can tell 'tighten the rubric and re-run' from 'this item is legitimately ambiguous, leave it dropped.' This is honest-surface output, not a new gate.

findings.claim: For very small expert studies, spreadsheets are explicitly endorsed as sufficient -- 'for very small projects, a spreadsheet, lightweight open-source tool, or custom script may be sufficient' -- while the documented downsides of spreadsheets (error-prone, no built-in QC, no progress tracking) are precisely the contract requirements that can instead be enforced at the export/ingest boundary rather than inside the editing surface.
findings.source: Industry annotation-tooling guidance: HumanSignal 2024, '10 Tips to Supercharge Your Data Labeling Efficiency' (https://humansignal.com/blog/10-tips-to-supercharge-your-data-labeling-efficiency/); Label Your Data 2026, 'Data Annotation Tools' (https://labelyourdata.com/articles/data-annotation/tools); arXiv:2403.07762 (Supporting Annotators with Affordances) on where custom UIs add value (recall/consistency on complex conversational labeling).
findings.design_implication: For a >=3-annotator / >=30-item one-shot expert round in a 1-human studio, a CSV/JSON round-trip is the correct leanness IF the export/ingest glue enforces what a spreadsheet cannot. The single exception is split-knowledge for catch items (Finding 3): the editing surface must not contain the answer key. Resolve by export producing TWO files -- a per-annotator gold-stripped items file (the only thing handed out) plus a private answer_key.json (shown_order + catch map + gold, kept by the director). A thin local single-file HTML form becomes worth it ONLY if external reviewers can't be trusted to leave columns alone, since the form can hide the key and lock shown_order; otherwise it is over-build.

findings.claim: Blinding to gold, to which side any model chose, and to candidate provenance is required to avoid anchoring/provenance bias, and the comparative A/B pick should be preceded by a per-side pointwise correctness micro-judgment because pairwise-only judging amplifies superficial-attribute bias.
findings.source: van der Lee et al. 2019, 'Best Practices for the Human Evaluation of Automatically Generated Text' (INLG 2019, ACL W19-8643, https://aclanthology.org/W19-8643/); Jeong et al. 2024, 'The Comparative Trap / PRePair' (arXiv:2406.12319, https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.12319); Kiritchenko & Mohammad 2017 (arXiv:1712.01765) on comparative judgments being more intra-annotator-consistent than Likert.
findings.design_implication: Blinding is an EXPORT-TIME transform, not a UI feature: the exporter must strip from each row the gold field, any model_id/provenance, and any record of which side a model picked -- producing rows that contain only the prompt + the two candidate texts in shown_order + an empty verdict cell. Because human_labels.py already passes items in (gold is grading-side only) and the loader never exposes gold, the missing piece is purely the export serializer that guarantees these fields are absent from the handed-out file. Add an optional per-side correctness column (good_A / good_B in {yes,no,unsure}) to capture the pointwise micro-judgment before the comparative pick; it is extra signal the loader can ignore today and consume later.

domains.recommendation: Build a CSV/JSON round-trip, NOT an app -- but make the export/ingest GLUE the enforcement layer, because the three hard requirements (order persistence, blinding, attention checks) are machine guarantees that a hand-edited spreadsheet cannot keep. Concretely, Epic 3 should ship two commands in the characterize package alongside human_labels.py: (1) `export-labels` -- takes the calibration items + the annotator roster, and per annotator emits a gold-stripped, provenance-stripped, shuffled items file (one row = prompt + candidate_A_text + candidate_B_text in a PERSISTED shown_order + empty verdict cell whose only legal values are A/B/unsure), interleaving a configurable fraction of gold-known CATCH items at randomized positions; it writes the items file (the only thing handed out) PLUS a private answer_key.json holding {row_id -> shown_order, is_catch, expected_side, true_item_id}. (2) `ingest-labels` -- reads the filled files + answer_key.json, inverts shown_order to recover true verdicts, computes each annotator's catch-pass rate and DROPS/flags annotators below a configurable floor BEFORE building records, validates the verdict enum (reject typos, treat unsure/tie as no-label), then emits exactly the human_labels.json shape load_human_labels already consumes (annotators+tiers / labels matrix). The ingest summary must report per-annotator catch-pass rate, Krippendorff alpha, the three disagreement classes, and the existing notes (epsilon clamp, <30-item underpower, DISPUTED drops). The split-knowledge two-file export is the one non-negotiable that forbids a single hand-filled spreadsheet. A thin single-file local HTML form is justified ONLY if untrusted external reviewers might edit the key or re-sort rows; for the director-plus-trusted-crew default it is over-build. Reuse, don't duplicate: the exporter must emit the SAME closed verdict enum and tier vocabulary the loader already enforces (A/B/PASS/FAIL + unsure set; expert/skilled/crowd), so the round-trip is byte-compatible with load_human_labels and PIN_PER_STEP holds.
domains.uncertainty: Three genuine open questions. (1) Catch-item budget: the <3% don't-know rate (Beck 2022) and Snow 2008's redundancy are crowd findings; for a >=30-item EXPERT round, a high catch fraction wastes scarce expert items against the >=30 alt-test floor (research-grounding 12.3's IRT screen already keeps only ~28/93 discriminators, so item budget is tight). I could not find a sourced catch-fraction rule for small expert studies specifically -- likely 10-20% with at least 3-4 catch items per annotator, but this needs a pilot, not a citation. (2) Whether the per-side pointwise micro-judgment (PRePair) should be MANDATORY in the form or optional: it adds annotator load on an already item-starved round, and human_labels.py does not consume it yet, so shipping it as an optional column risks unused complexity. (3) The single-file-HTML-vs-two-CSV decision hinges on a trust assumption about external reviewers that only the director can make; the literature says spreadsheets are 'sufficient for very small projects' but is silent on the split-knowledge edge case for catch items, which is the actual forcing function. If external reviewers are ever in the loop, revisit toward a thin form that holds answer_key.json server-side.

domains.question: ai-crucible IRT keeps 28 of 93 items as discriminators, but the alt-test wants at least 30. Which subset should humans label to validate the judge panel?

findings.claim: The alt-test prescribes at least 3 annotators on a 50 to 100 item subset (omega at or above 0.5, BY-FDR); the current at-least-30 floor is below the method own 50.
findings.source: Calderon, Reichart, Dror 2025 alt-test, arXiv:2501.10970 (verbatim: subset between 50 and 100, at least three annotators).
findings.design_implication: Target 50 to 100. Raise human_labels.py floor 30 to 50; keep per-tier epsilon and BY-FDR in metrics.alt_test.

findings.claim: Saturated items give near-zero signal (the prune dropped 36 of 93); anchors recover full-bank rank from about 100 items; growing the bank lifted discriminators 15 to 28 (51 to 93 items). Krippendorff alpha is unstable below about 50.
findings.source: Maia Polo et al. 2024 tinyBenchmarks arXiv:2402.14992; Peiyu Li et al. 2025 ATLAS arXiv:2511.04689; Krippendorff 2011; research-grounding.md 12.1, 12.3.
findings.design_implication: Reject b; commit to d. The 28 are the mandatory core; author hard items until the screen keeps at least 50 alone, then re-prune. Report alpha with a bootstrap CI; escalate if drops push N under 50.

findings.claim: A validation set must cover the construct domain (co-equal to discrimination), judged by at least 3 experts (Lynn); at small N, IRT or difficulty selection does not beat stratified random.
findings.source: Lynn 1986; Lawshe 1975 CVR; Boateng et al. 2018 https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6004510/ ; Micro-Benchmarking arXiv:2510.08730.
findings.design_implication: Favor stratified (c): set equals 28 discriminators plus a difficulty-by-construct top-up to 50 to 60 covering pruned constructs. Tag construct and difficulty in meta.json; treat a near-tie omega between two seats as non-separable.

domains.recommendation: Use a plus c plus d, never b. The 28 discriminators are the mandatory core; stratify a top-up to 50 to 60 covering pruned constructs; expand the bank to 50-plus discriminators.
domains.uncertainty: 50 is a floor not a guarantee; whether the 28 already cover all constructs is unverified; and the alt-test was validated on NLP and vision data rather than A/B unsure verdicts.
